description:
Worktrees were being created relative to the current working directory rather than the main git repo root. When coordinator spawned implementers for beads dr2.3 and dr2.4 from within the dr2.2 worktree, those sub-worktrees landed at `<dr2.2-worktree>/.claude/worktrees/` instead of `<main-repo>/.claude/worktrees/`. When the dr2.2 worktree was later removed (via /merged), its entire directory tree was deleted, orphaning dr2.3 and dr2.4's worktrees.

**Root cause:** The `.claude/worktrees/` base directory is resolved from CWD at time of spawn, not from the main repo root.

**Fix:** Before creating a worktree, resolve the base directory using:
```bash
git rev-parse --git-common-dir
# or
git -C <any-path> rev-parse --path-format=absolute --git-common-dir
```
The common git dir always points back to the main repo's `.git/`, regardless of which worktree you're in. Strip the trailing `/.git` and append `/.claude/worktrees/` to get the correct base.

**Files to inspect:**
- `.claude/skills/coordinator/SKILL.md` — the instruction telling coordinator where to create worktrees (via Agent tool `isolation: "worktree"` — this is handled by Claude Code itself, not a script)
- `.claude/skills/implementer/SKILL.md` — implementer receives a worktree path; check if it ever creates sub-worktrees
- `.claude/skills/merged/SKILL.md` — verify cleanup only removes the specific bead's worktree, not the whole directory

**Note:** The `isolation: "worktree"` parameter on the Agent tool is managed by Claude Code's runtime, not by our skill scripts. The actual worktree path returned to the coordinator comes from Claude Code. We need to verify whether the nesting happened due to: (a) the Agent tool being called from within a worktree context, causing Claude Code to nest worktrees, or (b) some explicit path construction in our skills.
title: Fix worktree nesting: always create worktrees under main repo root, never under another worktree

description:
## Summary
Enable multi-machine collaboration by adding Dolt remote sync to the agent workflow. Multiple developers/agents on different machines can share a single beads database via DoltHub, with transparent push/pull hooks so skills don't need to change.

## Subtasks
1. `/setup` slash command — one-time per-project remote configuration
2. Sync hooks — transparent PreToolUse/PostToolUse Claude Code hooks for dolt pull/push
3. Documentation — AGENTS.md section on multi-machine collaboration
title: Dolt remote sync for multi-machine collaboration

description:
## Summary
Create a `/setup-remote` slash command that guides the user through configuring a DoltHub remote for multi-machine beads collaboration. This is a one-time-per-project operation.

## Files to modify
- `NEW: .claude/commands/setup-remote.md` — the slash command definition

## Implementation steps
1. Create `.claude/commands/setup-remote.md` with instructions for the agent to:
   a. Ask the user if they want to configure a Dolt remote
   b. Ask for the DoltHub remote URL (e.g., `nguyenv119/my-project`)
   c. Check if `dolt` CLI is installed (`which dolt`)
   d. Check if `dolt login` has been run (look for `~/.dolt/creds/`)
   e. Run `(cd .beads/dolt && dolt remote add origin <url>)` to configure the remote
   f. Run `(cd .beads/dolt && dolt push -u origin main)` to seed the remote with current data
   g. Confirm success to the user

## Acceptance criteria
- Running `/setup-remote` in a project with beads initialized guides user through DoltHub remote configuration
- After completion, `(cd .beads/dolt && dolt remote -v)` shows the configured origin
- The remote has the current beads data (`dolt push` succeeded)
title: /setup-remote slash command for Dolt remote configuration

description:
## Summary
Add Claude Code hooks that transparently sync the local Dolt database with a DoltHub remote before reads and after writes. This makes multi-machine collaboration work without changing any skill files.

## Files to modify
- `NEW: .claude/hooks/bd-dolt-pull.sh` — PreToolUse hook script
- `NEW: .claude/hooks/bd-dolt-push.sh` — PostToolUse hook script
- `MODIFY: .claude/settings.json` — add PreToolUse and PostToolUse hook entries

## Implementation steps
1. Create `.claude/hooks/bd-dolt-pull.sh`:
   - Read stdin JSON, extract `tool_input.command` via `jq`
   - Exit 0 immediately if command doesn't start with `bd`
   - Locate the Dolt directory: `DOLT_DIR="$(bd where 2>/dev/null)/dolt"` (note: `bd where` returns the `.beads` dir)
   - Check if a Dolt remote named `origin` exists via `(cd "$DOLT_DIR" && dolt remote -v 2>/dev/null | grep -q origin)`; exit 0 if not
   - Run `(cd "$DOLT_DIR" && dolt pull origin main 2>/dev/null)` — use subshell+cd because `dolt` does NOT support a `-C` flag
   - Always exit 0 (never block the bd command)

2. Create `.claude/hooks/bd-dolt-push.sh`:
   - Read stdin JSON, extract `tool_input.command` via `jq`
   - **Use inverted allowlist**: define read-only commands (`show`, `list`, `ready`, `search`, `query`, `count`, `diff`, `history`, `status`, `types`, `blocked`, `stale`, `find-duplicates`, `lint`, `where`, `version`, `help`, `doctor`, `prime`, `recall`, `preflight`, `children`, `orphans`). If the bd subcommand is in this list, exit 0 (no push needed). Otherwise, push.
   - Locate the Dolt directory: `DOLT_DIR="$(bd where 2>/dev/null)/dolt"`
   - Check if remote exists (same as pull script)
   - Check if there are uncommitted changes: `(cd "$DOLT_DIR" && dolt status)`. Only add+commit if there are changes (guards against dolt auto-commit mode being `on`, where bd already committed).
   - Run `(cd "$DOLT_DIR" && dolt add . && dolt commit -m "bd sync" && dolt push origin main 2>/dev/null)` — all in a subshell because dolt has no `-C` flag
   - Always exit 0

3. Make both scripts executable (`chmod +x`)

4. Add to `.claude/settings.json`:
   - PreToolUse hook with matcher "Bash" pointing to `.claude/hooks/bd-dolt-pull.sh` (timeout: 30)
   - PostToolUse hook with matcher "Bash" pointing to `.claude/hooks/bd-dolt-push.sh` (timeout: 30)

## Key design decisions
- **No `dolt -C` flag**: dolt doesn't support it (unlike git). All dolt commands must use `(cd <dir> && dolt ...)` subshells.
- **`bd where` returns `.beads/` dir**: so Dolt dir is `$(bd where)/dolt`, NOT `$(bd where)/../dolt`.
- **Inverted allowlist for writes**: instead of listing every write command (which grows as bd adds features), we list read-only commands and push after everything else. More future-proof.
- **Dirty-check before commit**: if `dolt-auto-commit` is `on`, bd already commits. The hook checks `dolt status` to avoid empty commits.
- **Matcher is "Bash"**: Claude Code matchers filter on tool name only. The scripts filter on command prefix internally.
- **No-op when no remote**: local-only setups are completely unaffected.
- **Always exit 0**: never block agent work on network failures.

## Acceptance criteria
- With a remote configured: `bd create "test"` triggers a pull before and push after
- Without a remote: `bd create "test"` works exactly as before (no latency added)
- Network failures are silently ignored (exit 0)
- Non-bd Bash commands are not affected (script exits immediately)
- If dolt auto-commit is on, the push hook doesn't fail on empty commits
title: Transparent Dolt sync hooks for bd commands

description:
## Summary
Add a "Multi-Machine Collaboration" section to AGENTS.md documenting how to use Dolt remote sync for team-based agent workflows.

## Files to modify
- `MODIFY: AGENTS.md` — add new section after "Issue Tracking with bd"

## Implementation steps
1. Add a "## Multi-Machine Collaboration" section to AGENTS.md covering:
   a. What it does: multiple developers/agents share a single beads database via DoltHub
   b. How to set it up: run `/setup` and provide DoltHub URL
   c. How it works: Claude Code hooks auto-pull before `bd` reads, auto-push after `bd` writes
   d. Onboarding new team members: `dolt login`, clone the project repo, hooks auto-sync
   e. Limitations: Dolt merge conflicts possible if two machines push competing changes simultaneously (resolve like git conflicts)
2. Keep the section concise — this is a reference doc, not a tutorial

## Acceptance criteria
- AGENTS.md has a clear section explaining multi-machine collaboration
- A new team member reading AGENTS.md would know how to set up and use remote sync
title: Documentation: multi-machine collaboration in AGENTS.md

description:
## Summary
Update README.md and EXAMPLE.md to reflect the three-layer standards loading architecture. The Architecture tree in README needs `standards/` and the reviewer/implementer descriptions should mention standards loading. Keep same style and length.

## Files to modify
- `README.md` — update Architecture tree to include `standards/`, update skills description
- `EXAMPLE.md` — mention that reviewers and implementer load shared standards before starting

## Implementation steps
1. In README.md Architecture section, add `standards/` under `skills/` with description
2. In README.md, update the `skills/` description to mention standards
3. In EXAMPLE.md, add a brief mention in the implementer/reviewer steps that they load shared standards
4. Keep same style, tone, and approximate length — no verbose additions

## Acceptance criteria
- Architecture tree accurately reflects current directory structure
- Standards loading is mentioned but not over-explained
- Same concise style as existing content
title: Update README.md and EXAMPLE.md to reflect standards restructuring

description: Create a minimal FastAPI application to test the end-to-end /work workflow. Include a health check endpoint, one data endpoint, basic tests, and all standard project scaffolding (requirements.txt, README).
title: Build trivial FastAPI app

description:
## Summary

Enhance the 3 reviewer skills (correctness, architecture, tests) with investigation methodology and review discipline guardrails, based on a comparison with Devin's code review prompt. Two independent analyses converged on the same 5 gaps.

## Files to modify

- `.claude/skills/reviewer-correctness/SKILL.md` — Add Step 1.5 "Investigate Context" (git log, grep callers, git show before-state); add § G reference in Step 4
- `.claude/skills/reviewer-architecture/SKILL.md` — Add Step 2.5 "Evaluate the Approach" (right solution?, root cause vs symptom, adversarial thinking); add `quality.md` to Step 0; add § G reference in Step 4
- `.claude/skills/reviewer-tests/SKILL.md` — Add § G reference in review process section
- `.claude/skills/standards/quality.md` — Add section G "Review Discipline" with 3 subsections: G1 What NOT to Flag, G2 False-Positive Discipline, G3 Output Prioritization

## Implementation steps

1. Add section G to `standards/quality.md` (after section F) with:
   - G1: Don't flag style, preferences, hypotheticals, or uncertain findings
   - G2: "Delete any finding you're not confident about. False positives waste everyone's time." + verification questions
   - G3: Most important first, max 5 non-trivial per review, every finding needs file:line
2. Add Step 1.5 to `reviewer-correctness/SKILL.md` between Steps 1 and 2:
   - `git log --oneline -10 -- <changed-files>`
   - `git show <base-branch>:<filepath>` for before state
   - `grep -r "function_name"` for callers
   - Three questions: why written this way, who calls this, what assumptions encoded
3. Add Step 2.5 to `reviewer-architecture/SKILL.md` between Steps 1 and 2:
   - Is this the right solution? Root cause or symptom?
   - Simpler way? What could go wrong?
   - "If the approach itself is wrong, say so clearly"
4. Add `quality.md` to `reviewer-architecture/SKILL.md` Step 0 load list
5. Add "See quality.md § G" reference to verify/report sections of all 3 reviewers
6. Read each modified file end-to-end to verify section numbering consistency

## Plan file

See `.claude/plans/cheerful-nibbling-anchor.md` for full context.
title: Enhance reviewer skills with Devin-inspired investigation & discipline

description: Replace isolation: worktree with manual git worktree add for persistent worktrees. Add --repo flag derived from origin to all gh commands. Worktrees live as siblings of the main repo dir.
title: Fix coordinator: manual persistent worktrees and --repo flag for gh commands

description:
## Summary
Add explicit "real over mock" testing guidelines to the implementer and test reviewer skills. Current skills mention mocks but don't establish a clear hierarchy: real > in-memory > mock.

## Files to modify
- `.claude/skills/implementer/SKILL.md` — Add a "Mock Discipline" section under Principles with the 4 rules
- `.claude/skills/reviewer-tests/SKILL.md` — Add corresponding review checks for mock discipline violations

## Implementation steps
1. In implementer SKILL.md, add a "Mock Discipline" subsection under Principles with these rules:
   - Before writing any mock, ask: "Can I use the real thing?"
   - If a function is trivial glue (e.g., a 5-line fetch wrapper), don't unit-test it — test at the integration layer
   - Use the factory pattern for routers/services so tests can inject in-memory databases
   - Mocks are acceptable ONLY when the real dependency is genuinely unavailable (e.g., Chrome API in jsdom, third-party SaaS API)
2. In reviewer-tests SKILL.md, add a "Mock Discipline" review section that flags:
   - Mocks of dependencies that could be replaced with real or in-memory alternatives
   - Unit tests of trivial glue code that should be integration tests instead
   - Missing factory patterns that would enable dependency injection
   - Mocks of available dependencies without justification

## Acceptance criteria
- Implementer skill has clear mock discipline rules under Principles
- Test reviewer skill checks for mock discipline violations and flags them
- Existing content is not duplicated — new rules complement what's already there
title: Add real-over-mock testing guidelines to implementer and test reviewer skills

description: Before spawning implementers, resolve true main repo root via git worktree list. Ensure worktrees are created as siblings of main root, not nested. Add anti-pattern for creating worktrees while CWD is inside another worktree.
title: Fix coordinator worktree creation to resolve main repo root first
